title: U.S. Government & Civics
description: Branches of government, Supreme Court cases, founding documents, and civic knowledge

cards.back: Legislative, Executive, Judicial
Congress (Senate + House of Representatives) — makes laws
President, Vice President, Cabinet — enforces laws
Supreme Court and federal courts — interprets laws
Congress (Legislative branch)
The President (Executive branch)
The Supreme Court (Judicial branch)
100 (2 per state)
435 (based on state population)
6 years
2 years
4 years
The Vice President
Presides over the House of Representatives; 2nd in presidential succession
Body of 538 electors who formally elect the President; 270 needed to win

cards.front: How many electoral votes to win the presidency?
Checks and balances — what is it?
cards.back: System where each branch limits the power of the other branches
President's power to reject a bill passed by Congress

cards.front: How can Congress override a veto?
cards.back: Two-thirds vote in both the House and Senate
Power of the Supreme Court to declare laws unconstitutional
Marbury v. Madison (1803)
Established judicial review — Supreme Court can strike down unconstitutional laws
Declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional
Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
Established a right to abortion (later overturned by Dobbs, 2022)
Required police to inform suspects of their rights before questioning
Miranda v. Arizona (1966)
Upheld 'separate but equal' racial segregation (later overturned by Brown)
Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)
Right to an attorney even if you cannot afford one

cards.front: What case guaranteed right to a lawyer?
cards.back: Gideon v. Wainwright (1963)
Students do not lose free speech rights at school (armband protest)
Ruled enslaved people were not citizens; widely condemned, overturned by 14th Amendment
Established federal supremacy and implied powers (Congress can create a national bank)
System of shared power between national and state governments
The first 10 amendments to the Constitution
The Bill of Rights
Article VI — federal law is the 'supreme law of the land'
Allows Congress to make laws needed to carry out its powers
Right to appear before a judge and not be imprisoned without cause
House of Representatives impeaches; Senate holds the trial
Extended debate in the Senate to delay or block a vote

cards.front: How many amendments to the Constitution?
Who wrote the Declaration of Independence?
cards.back: Thomas Jefferson (primary author)
1787 (Constitutional Convention in Philadelphia)

cards.front: The Federalist Papers — what are they?
cards.back: 85 essays by Hamilton, Madison, and Jay arguing for ratification of the Constitution
Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay
